feed.title: Pronounce — recent dictionary additions
feed.subtitle: How engineers actually pronounce project / product / jargon names. Community-maintained.

entry.title: MAI — M A I
entry.summary: MAI is pronounced "M A I" (/ˌɛm eɪ ˈaɪ/). Microsoft AI's in-house model family (MAI-Thinking-1, MAI-Voice-2, ...); read letter-by-letter like the "AI" it contains — Microsoft has published no official pronunciation.

entry.title: Corsair — kor sair
entry.summary: Corsair is pronounced "kor sair" (/ˈkɔːrsɛər/). d-Matrix's in-memory-compute inference accelerator (also the PC-hardware brand); American dictionaries stress the first syllable — KOR-sair, not the French-style cor-SAIR.

entry.title: lighttpd — lie tee
entry.summary: lighttpd is pronounced "lie tee" (/ˈlaɪti/). Portmanteau of light + httpd; the official site states it is pronounced "lighty".

entry.title: Immich — image
entry.summary: Immich is pronounced "image" (/ˈɪmɪdʒ/). Self-hosted photo/video backup server; creator Alex Tran: "It is pronounced as 'image'", though much of the community says IMM-itch.

entry.title: Debezium — dee bee zee um
entry.summary: Debezium is pronounced "dee bee zee um" (/diˈbiːziəm/). Official FAQ: "dee-BEE-zee-uhm" — say "DBs-ium" fast; from DBs + the periodic-table -ium suffix.

entry.title: NiFi — nigh fie
entry.summary: NiFi is pronounced "nigh fie" (/ˈnaɪˌfaɪ/). Apache dataflow tool from NSA's "NiagaraFiles"; the official FAQ states "nye fye" is the preferred pronunciation.

entry.title: Keptn — captain
entry.summary: Keptn is pronounced "captain" (/ˈkæptən/). CNCF application life-cycle orchestration project (archived Sept 2025); official glossary states "Keptn, pronounced captain."

entry.title: Tyk — tyke
entry.summary: Tyk is pronounced "tyke" (/taɪk/). Open-source Go API gateway; creator Martin Buhr confirmed on GitHub it rhymes with "Mike" — /taɪk/.

entry.title: Xapian — zap ee un
entry.summary: Xapian is pronounced "zap ee un" (/ˈzæpiən/). FAQ: usually "ZAP-ian" like the word zap; "ZAY-pian" is a popular variant (name mangles "sapient").

entry.title: Kueue — kyoo
entry.summary: Kueue is pronounced "kyoo" (/kjuː/). Kubernetes-native job-queueing controller (kubernetes-sigs); the K-wordplay name is read exactly like "queue" — one syllable, kyoo.

entry.title: Linode — lin ode
entry.summary: Linode is pronounced "lin ode" (/ˈlɪnoʊd/). Cloud host (acquired by Akamai in 2022; now Akamai Connected Cloud), from "Linux node"; the official @linode account clarified "We pronounce it Lih-node - 'lin' as in Linux!", though LIE-node remains a common mispronunciation.

entry.title: OpenBao — open bao
entry.summary: OpenBao is pronounced "open bao" (/ˌoʊpən ˈbaʊ/). Linux Foundation fork of HashiCorp Vault named after baozi, the steamed bun (per GitHub discussion #34) — "bao" rhymes with "cow", not "bay-oh".

entry.title: Sqitch — skitch
entry.summary: Sqitch is pronounced "skitch" (/skɪtʃ/). Wheeler's database change-management tool ("SQL changes with Git stuck in the middle"); pronounced "skitch", not "skwitch".

entry.title: Guice — juice
entry.summary: Guice is pronounced "juice" (/dʒuːs/). Google's Java dependency-injection framework; the README opens with "Guice (pronounced 'juice')".

entry.title: Thymeleaf — time leaf
entry.summary: Thymeleaf is pronounced "time leaf" (/ˈtaɪmˌliːf/). Java/Spring server-side template engine; the official FAQ spells it out as /ˈtaɪmˌlɪːf/ — th sounds like plain t, as in the herb thyme (TIME-leaf).

entry.title: Vitess — vih tess
entry.summary: Vitess is pronounced "vih tess" (/vɪˈtɛs/). CNCF-graduated MySQL sharding system from YouTube; named after French "vitesse" (speed), so stress falls on the second syllable.

entry.title: musl — muscle
entry.summary: musl is pronounced "muscle" (/ˈmʌsəl/). Lightweight libc behind Alpine Linux images; official site says "musl, pronounced like the word 'mussel' or 'muscle'".

entry.title: axios — ak see ose
entry.summary: axios is pronounced "ak see ose" (/ˈæksioʊs/). Promise-based HTTP client among npm's most-downloaded packages; from Greek axios ("worthy") with no official ruling — top-voted community reading is "AK-see-ohs".

entry.title: Mockito — mah kee toh
entry.summary: Mockito is pronounced "mah kee toh" (/mɑːˈkiːtoʊ/). Java mocking framework whose name is a maintainer-confirmed pun on mock + mojito; a maintainer answered issue #889 with mock-based /mɑˈkiːtoʊ/ ("mah-KEE-toh"), while creator Szczepan Faber told the mailing list to "go ahead and pronounce it the mojito way" ("moh-KEE-toh"), so the split is genuine.

entry.title: xonsh — kahnsh
entry.summary: xonsh is pronounced "kahnsh" (/kɑːnʃ/). Python-powered cross-platform shell; the official homepage states it "sounds like 'consh'".

entry.title: Pyodide — pie uh dyed
entry.summary: Pyodide is pronounced "pie uh dyed" (/ˈpaɪəˌdaɪd/). CPython compiled to WebAssembly for running Python in the browser; maintainers rth and hoodmane gave the IPA ˈpɑɪ.ə.ˌdɑɪd (Py + Iodide — rhymes with iodide).

entry.title: Haxe — hex
entry.summary: Haxe is pronounced "hex" (/hɛks/). Cross-platform language compiling to JS, C++, JVM and more; early official docs said "hex" while English speakers naturally say "hacks" — no creator ruling, both survive at conferences.

entry.title: FLTK — full tick
entry.summary: FLTK is pronounced "full tick" (/ˈfʊltɪk/). Veteran cross-platform C++ GUI toolkit; the official docs open with 'The Fast Light Tool Kit ("FLTK", pronounced "fulltick")'.

entry.title: Cygwin — sig win
entry.summary: Cygwin is pronounced "sig win" (/ˈsɪɡwɪn/). POSIX compatibility layer for Windows (Cygnus + Windows); Wikipedia gives /ˈsɪɡwɪn/ SIG-win, confirmed by a Cygwin developer on the project mailing list.

entry.title: Nagios — nah ghee ose
entry.summary: Nagios is pronounced "nah ghee ose" (/ˈnɑːɡiːoʊs/). Veteran open-source infrastructure monitoring system (NetSaint 1999, renamed 2002); creator Ethan Galstad's official FAQ spells it out as "nah-ghee-ose" — "Nah" plus the start of "geese" plus the end of "verbose" — so the g is hard, never "NAY-jee-os", and Wikipedia records the same /ˈnɑːɡiːoʊs/.
